ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper presents a new RL environment (RLE) that extends ALE-style benchmarks to SNES/Genesis and many consoles via LibRetro (supporting thousands of ROMs) and adds multi-agent support and practical case studies (Sections 3.2–3.4, 4.3). This is primarily an engineering/platform contribution that is clearly differentiated from prior platforms (ALE, OpenAI Gym/Universe, Malmo, DeepMind Lab) but does not introduce new learning algorithms or theory—rather it raises new empirical challenges (3D, delayed reward, large action spaces). Overall this is a useful and moderately novel resource for the community but is incremental rather than a fundamentally new methodological advance.

CLARITY.justification: The paper has a clear high-level organization (Intro, Related Work, Environment, Experiments, Conclusion) and the main contributions are explicitly listed early in the Introduction. However, presentation quality is uneven: notation/equations are presented but contain formatting issues and minor inconsistencies (e.g., the Q-update line and misplaced footnotes), and many figures/tables are referenced but missing or shown as placeholders (e.g., Figure (), Table (3)). The writing suffers from repeated/duplicated paragraphs, LaTeX artifacts, and some grammatical slips, which make parts harder to follow and reduce overall readability.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper's main claims (introducing RLE, demonstrating training difficulties on SNES titles, reward-shaping benefits, and that alternating-opponent training can improve robustness) are clearly stated and supported by illustrative experiments (e.g. F‑Zero and Super Mario reward‑shaping in §4.2, Mortal Kombat multi‑agent experiments in §4.3). Mathematical content is minimal and the presented DQN update is standard and essentially correct. However, experimental methodology lacks important details and rigor (only a small/unspecified set of games tested, no hyperparameter/run/seed reporting, no variance or statistical tests, and limited description of action‑space pruning), so many claims (generalization across games, superiority of the opponent‑ensemble method) are demonstrated only empirically on narrow cases and without sufficient reproducibility metrics.
